segments.title: Stalin entering the London Treaty changes the naval equation
segments.summary: The Patreon stream frames Soviet entry into the 1930 London Treaty as a fragile counterfactual driven by Stalin's demands, security concerns, and treaty-limit incentives.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing premise. Dr. Clarke starts by warning that Stalin's presence could itself collapse the treaty, then treats him as a more demanding version of the Japanese treaty problem whose participation would reshape British and Soviet assumptions. The later discussion narrows the motive set to ego, interest, and need, then argues that even without signing the Soviets still had reasons to care about treaty limits and escalator clauses.

evidence.note: The opening premise says the treaty might collapse from Stalin's presence and that treating him like Japan would still leave a more demanding Soviet participant.
The discussion identifies ego, interest, and need as the possible reasons Stalin would attend the London Naval Treaty.
The transcript links Stalin's priorities to security and argues the Soviets were affected by treaty limits and escalator clauses even if outside the agreement.
